speaker: interviewer
text: The interview with Ruth Krol. It's the 25th of September, 1995. So Ruth, your bosses fled into Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to this old man's place.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were a few days. That was just the front line. You heard the whistling of the bullets and it was, you know, state of war there. And then it was absolutely quiet, and I heard the steps of soldiers. And I was only, sort of, a foreigner. So who opened the door? Of course, they knocked on the door. They most likely saw the chimney was working, that somebody was making smoke--

words.text: you know, state of war there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The smoke from the chimney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody's cooking there. So they knocked on the door. And I opened because I thought they wouldn't do me any harm, that they'd actually come and save me. So I opened the door. And they speak-- I also have before me. There was a Russian Mongol. With the funny--

speaker: interviewee
text: A man from Mongolia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Very tall with a pistol, huge. Pushing it toward my breast and pushing me down the stairs into the center. And you want it, you know, there is a wild. And I saw the whole picture before me. The consequences. And I took my leg and pushed him in the part which really hurt. And he was-- and I saw whether the pistol will go off. You know that is, I thought that I wouldn't be alive anymore. And it didn't. So I ran up the steps, and he was with two other soldiers. They were European. Actually, they were Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said in Polish, what does he want from me? I'm Polish. I'm not a German. And they said, quickly to the wardrobe. Hide, and we will pull him out. So that was really-- you know, that he didn't shoot-- maybe he was in so much pain. I wouldn't know. I was absolutely convinced that that is it. So that was a horrifying experience. So they got him away.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got him away. And then, different Russian soldiers came with women. And some were good. They gave. The others took away. They were very keen on watches. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And very keen on women. So I was lucky I worked before in the station. And lots of French people knew me. And there were actually five French boys who wanted also not to go inside-- only to wait up for what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now these French people had been sent to the area as forced labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: To labor. As forced labor, yes. And one fellow was especially nice to me and said, don't worry. I'll hide you. I will say you're my wife. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it in my working book. Andre was the first name. But I have the full name-- I didn't have anything else like my working book, so he wrote it there in my working book.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this Andre said, don't worry, I will--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll save you because you are my wife. So he did that very nicely. He protected me always. And we tried to go towards Poland. Not to stay in Germany, because in Poland, already, they had prepared food for the people who came from Germany, and so on. So I started to go with them toward Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you say with them--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the five French.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were five?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five boys who kept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were the five Frenchmen and you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And there were soup kitchens, really. And we went into such a soup kitchen. And in the corner, in the table, I saw a fellow with the stripes, how they wear in the concentration camp. And my heart said it's true. And I said to him, could you wait a moment? I just want to speak to the man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to him, and said, are you Jewish? Yes. And I said, so am I. And I said, where have you been? He was in the camp. But he was sick. He was in the sick station. And they went in such a hurry, when the Russians came, that he was left behind. So with him, I knew the Jews are still alive. Maybe not many, but the Jews are alive. So you realized that maybe there were going to be some people left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. And some Jews left. And I said to Andre, look, I like you very much, but I am a Jew. He didn't know that before. And I belong to my people. And you don't take it wrong. I won't come with you to France. I've got to go home, and I've got to see who is left from my people. And he was very upset, but young. So what could it have hurt him so much?

speaker: interviewer
text: He wanted to marry you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He wanted to take me to France with him. Anyway, I went quite a bit through hell, because, by foot, I went to my hometown. To Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went from Oels to Katowice.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Oels I walked to Katowice. And that was very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't-- days. Nighttime we always-- like, the Germans slept in the Bahnhof station, and so we slept wherever we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the beginning of early 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, January.

speaker: interviewer
text: So wintertime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wintertime. Very cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I walked into-- with not very warm clothes. I remembered, somewhere, I had my shoes completely soaked. So a Russian gave me his. They had such field-- high shoes. But not out of leather, but very thick and very warm. So I walked back in these shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a Russian gave you warm boots, or something--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sort of their boots. And well, when I came towards Katowice, I took a tram. The trams were already-- I didn't have any money to pay for it, but somehow it took me in. I said I come from the camp. So it took me into Katowice. And that, also, was a terrible shock. Our business was destroyed. The whole house was bombarded. And I was all on my own. I didn't know where to go. What to do. And so I got the idea that my girlfriend, they had a servant. And that girl lived in the same house. Like, had a little place. Still, when they went to the camps, they kept the place. And I had a look there. And really, she was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was your friend's servant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Friend's servant. And she took me in. But she was very worried, because it was a time, you know, like in war. It was still not said that the Russians and Poles are fully in. And there was always, well, what if the Germans came back?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see, at this stage, when you were in Katowice, did you see Germans anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No Germans anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans looked like they had gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: It looked like, but you know, they always, the population can come back-- airplanes constantly over the head, and you really didn't know what is what.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was a stage of chaos--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was an in between time. The Germans' military must have left. But it very often happens in a war that they come back. They conquer again. So the population was very worried. And then I sniffed a bit around. I said, my God, there must be Jewish people. Maybe some came back like me. And there was one. I met one lady. Her husband was a German. And he went for her into the camp so that she could stay back. And, of course, she was pleased to see a Jewish girl. I was pleased to see her. So from that time on, then slowly, slowly, trickled some men, also some women who ran away like me. Also who ran away during their transport into Germany. And they came there. And she sort of made like her home was for these people then. And we wrote out our name and who we were looking for. And I thought a bit of life started. A Jewish life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these were Jewish survivors who had somehow--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish survivors, like me, only they were in the camp. But through that, they took them in the inside then, towards the end. Everybody-- that is the way I lost my brother. From the camps, they took them right into the inside of Germany. And walking. And therefore, so many died on the way. And the bit who had guts, they ran away. Because it was chaos at the time.

words.text: they took them in the inside then,

speaker: interviewee
text: And your brother, just to go back to your brother for a moment-- he remained in the camp until--

speaker: interviewee
text: Until they had to go inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say inside, you mean back into Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Into Germany. And they brought him to Buchenwald. And for my-- after the war, for my restitution of my parents' properties, I had to find a way to tell them where my brother was, and I looked all over. And I got a German reply that he was in Buchenwald and he passed away on the 17th of April, 1945. So towards the end. That was terrible. That was my bad luck. I counted on that I'd keep him alive. And that really destroyed me.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact that, right at the end, he perished.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, I must tell you. I was, like in January '45, I was already in Katowice. And I worked in a restaurant. A friend of mine took a restaurant which their parents had, wasn't destroyed. So she said, give me a hand. So I was with her there. And what intuition means-- it must have been the time I lost my husband-- my brother, sorry. I was so terribly upset. Out of no reason, I started to cry. I was like-- that must have been the moment I lost my brother. When I think back now, that exactly-- because there really wasn't a reason to be suddenly so shaken. So there is a certain intuition in life, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened with this feeling that this-- sort of terrible sadness that came over you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was absolutely, for the day-- I couldn't do a thing. I was constantly crying. It wasn't-- later on, when I got the certificate from the Germans, I brought one and one together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the times coincided.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you remained in Katowice. You worked in this restaurant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the restaurant came lots of people who came back from the camp, also and there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Jewish restaurant? Or just a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It was more before the war, it was a Jewish restaurant. Then it was, nobody kept Kosher and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was just people coming into it. Jewish people started to drift back to it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was. Yes. And there was one gentleman, a Viennese doctor, who came in. And he was happy that I spoke German. Because at that time, it was hard to find the right people. And actually, from him, I can't tell you much, but he introduced me to my husband. And that was, from that time on, I had a happier life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where had your husband been? Just very briefly.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was also in the concentration camp, in Blechhammer, and that is a part of Auschwitz. And he was from-- maybe four years in different camps. And what made him survive, he was a dentist, and he was camp dentist. But he had a very, very awful life, of course. Harder than me, because he was a man. Because the men suffered much, much more. And he had the number on his arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Krol.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were introduced to Jan Krol by this person, this Viennese person, and when did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We liked each other at once. And we clicked very well. And I married him on the 25th of October-- was it October, yes. '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you met him, and you married very quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very quickly. And he was very good. He looked very much after me. He was good when I met him, and he was good when I lost him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a wonderful marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in Poland after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] remained-- I don't know, what would it be-- '46 or '47. Maybe to '47. I have an aunt in Paris, and they sent us the visas. The French visas. And we went to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I emigrated to Australia. That was-- I arrived here on the 3rd of December, '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in France for over two years. What did you do in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was a dentist on the [INAUDIBLE], which is actually-- was for Jewish refugees. Organized by American Jews, and I think the French government. They-- you know, they got the teeth repaired, and new teeth when necessary. And he was in the team for quite a few dentists. And in the meantime, I learned shirt-making. Because I didn't know where I will end up, and what I will need. So that was also very good. It was the same organization. We were there for a full day, and we had our lunch there. And, well, France is beautiful. We were poor, but we were very happy. Because of the whole surroundings. You know, the difference. You come from Germany. You come from Poland. And all of a sudden, you are in France. That is like day and night. The people are friendly. When somebody's drunk, he doesn't take the knife out and kill somebody else. You know, when they are drunk, they're happy. And so that is the whole attitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a completely different world.

speaker: interviewee
text: A different world, and what a nice world.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to come to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted-- I wanted, actually, to go to the States, where I had two aunts. But we fell to the Polish quota, because my husband was Polish. And we waited. We waited. And then, a friend of ours came and said, look. They're looking for people in Australia. But you must be a tailor. Tailors were wanted for Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my husband learned to sew straight. And I found here some far family of mine. And they sent us a paper. And it took us maybe three or four weeks. And we were called to the-- I think it was the English consulate, which worked for the Australians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it went very quickly. And also, to get on the boat was very easy because my uncle was in a quite high position, and he dictated who comes here, who comes there.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is your French aunt, the husband of the lady who lived in France.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And he was maybe happy that the Jewish wouldn't be anymore in France. You know, as I said, he was a good Catholic. And a very good de Gaullist. And they , actually, they were very-- they're not friends of the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: So your aunt had married a Frenchman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But with her looks, it was-- she looked so terribly German that nobody ever would have said that she's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in Australia in which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In December '49. 3rd December '49.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you've lived here ever since.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've lived here ever since, and I never ever for a minute regretted that I am here. It is a friendly country. It's got very little anti-Semitism. When you are willing to work, you can work. I was on the third day here and I found work in a restaurant. [INAUDIBLE]. On Margaret Street, that was once very famous. And my husband first worked in a factory. It didn't make him very happy, but then he worked with Dr. Wern, on [INAUDIBLE] and was very appreciated there, because Dr. Wern went on his trips, and he actually saw the whole surgery for him. And we established ourselves quickly because we worked so hard. We found the deposit for a house. And we went to Bankstown, where lots of foreigners were. Poles and all nationalities. And there is a big railway sort of yard, you know. And the Poles, especially, the Poles came to us, like to the church. Whatever they had-- my husband wasn't only the dentist for them. Whenever they had problems, he had to solve it. And he was very talented with languages. You had Ukrainians and Russians and whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very happy time for us. And he was so busy, that when, the first time, when he developed his sickness, and he had the cough, he was so busy, he didn't go to the doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, look, go buy me a cough mixture. He was so terribly busy, and never thinking about himself. Only about his work. And he only was happy studying his books. That's what really made him happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your husband got sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband got very sick. He developed asthma, and the asthma was treated with cortisone, and the cortisone had terrible side effects. And after we were fighting for eight years, I even went to Canada and sought help there, which really wasn't what we expected. And I lost him here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your husband die?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 13th of January, '74. He died one day before my birthday. And he had the birthday card on his desk, and he couldn't fill it in anymore. I have it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop there.
